speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 10, 1995. This is an interview with Milton Schwartz.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken when I was nine months old. The picture survived in America by my aunt Rachel. And I'll start to explain who the people are in the picture. From the left is my older sister, Ruchel. Then next comes my mother holding me when I was nine months old. In the back of my mother is my aunt Rachel, which she was immigrated to America in 1928. Then comes my father, Michael, and my older brother, Eliezer. And in the back of him is my uncle, Berl. He happened to be with our group in the woods. He was one of the 18 people in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Lutsk, where I was born in Lutsk. And this one, my mother told me I was nine months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was in about 1926, 1927?

speaker: interviewee
text: '27, probably. Yeah. This is a picture taken when I was 10 years old. From the left is my father. Then is my brother, Eliezer. Holding the wheelbarrow is my sister, Maya. She's here eight years old. Then sitting in a highchair is my brother, Abraham. He was six years old. My sister, Ruchel, in the back, she was 13 years old. And my mother-- and I was one-- on my knee, 10 years old. Which I am the only survivor of my whole family. After this picture was taken, a few years later, my mother gave birth to a little boy, David. He is not in the picture. And I don't have a picture of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- was this picture sent to America also?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was sent to America to my aunt. And my aunt retained it. And I got it from her. And I made another picture from it. So this is-- this how the picture survived. From the left is my mother's sister, my aunt Tova. She was married. She had two children. One child was 11. She survived. She was in a camp during the-- the summer of June, in a-- in a summer camp. And then when the war started, the Russians took all the summer camp children to Siberia, to Russia. And the older daughter survived. A younger son, [NON-ENGLISH]. He was six years old, was killed together with a mother and father. The father was Petrushka-- Pesach Petrushka, and Tova Petrushka, together, were killed. Then the middle one is Esther. She was married. She had a husband-- I forgot his name. She had a little child of four years old, a boy. They were killed together, all three, during the action of the ghetto. And the third girl, on the right, is my youngest aunt. She was Rachel. She was 19 years old. She was single-- she was never married, not married yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was probably taken in June the '30s, '38, '37. Because this is a picture that I specifically cannot put a finger or date. Because they were by my aunt's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken in Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's in Lutsk, where they were all living in Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sent to America also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sent to America, by mail to America. This is a picture of my older sister, Ruchel. The day when they killed her, the 20th of August 1942, she had two little children. One child was Louisa, and the other child was Fania. The youngest was nine months old, the oldest was about three years old. She was killed in the same time when they evacuated the ghetto, the 20th of August, 1942. She could have survived. But she came from a village to visit their mother, to see her mother. And she brought along with her the two children. When the farmer brought her into the ghetto, came in the evening to take her, they wouldn't let him in. The German army with the Ukrainians surrounded the area, and she perished this way in the ghetto. Her husband survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her husband, Kissel Aivainer survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture was taken in Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: In about what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Lutsk. My-- my brother-in-law carried with him a little picture. And it was reproduced in-- in Russia, a memory-- a remembrance to my-- my sister. My brother cherished this picture very much. This is my brother-in-law in the time when he was in the army. And he survived. And this picture was taken in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia. Yes. Russian uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you state his full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Kissel Aivainer He was a tailor. During the wartime, the Russian generals kept him in the Russian army, in order to make coats and uniforms for the generals and for their wives. For this they didn't send him into the war. And somehow he survived the war, too. Now, this is the way Kissel Aivainer looks now, my brother-in-law. He came to America in 1989, the 1st of January. He met a Russian woman from Romania. And he married there from Kishinev. And he married there, and he's living here. And needless to say he is very, very-- mourning the fact that he lost a wife and two children. They could have been now-- by now-- grandmothers and have their own children. He is living now in New York City and living in Queens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please note the many survivors who came from the war from the surrounding areas. Many of them have uniform on. These are the Jewish people commemorizing their loved ones. On the mass graves, they put up a memorial tablets.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the tablet was a star of David, written in Hebrew, and in Russian, and in Polish. But the Russian government apparently didn't like the Star of David. They destroyed it, and in turn, they put only a small stone. And on the stone was written down, there were Russian citizens buried in this area. They wouldn't even write down that in this Lutsk, 7 kilometers [NON-ENGLISH], that the Jewish people were buried there-- only, only because they were Jewish. No other reason and no other crime at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a picture taken in 1950, the gathering of the people who came to say memorial prayers was the day of the-- of the-- when the Germans started killing. They-- they retained this memorial Kaddish saying every year. So any survivors come there. It's a place where I went to say Kaddish in 1989. This is the mount of the burial place and the mass graves where 28,000 people were buried. And I am, myself, taking the picture. On this note, on the top of the mount, is a little small stone. And when I came to the place to say Kaddish in 1989, I found the stone was toppled because there's fresh cement under the stone. And it was written down, here the German shot 28,000 Russian citizens in the years of 1942, beginning of August 20. And then nothing cover it to say that this is a grave site of the strictly Jewish people. This is the day of my wedding with my wife Sarah in 1953, the 18th of August. This was, needless to say, the happiest day of my life. And we still hope that we'll celebrate the golden anniversary, coming eight years or so from now, God willing. Here is our beautiful four children. I'll start from the left-- the top is the beautiful daughter, Judy. She is an accounting major. She graduated accounting. She graduated from college. Then is my youngest son, Allen, the professor. He is a rabbi in the same time. He is a professor in Yeshiva University College. He is a rabbi in 95th Street in the west side of Manhattan. Then comes my daughter, Marlene-- she is getting her doctorate hopefully this year. And my son, the oldest son, Michael, he's an electrical engineer. And he is now 40 years old. And the age of the other children I didn't say, but anyway. My youngest son, Allen, is 34. He is six years younger than the oldest son. My younger daughter is 32. And my older daughter is 38. This is my oldest son, Michael's, family. Sully-- he is now about 10 years old; Michelle, Rena, the bat mitzvah girl. My son, Michael. He is here 38 years old. And Ari, the youngest son, he is here, seven years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken about a year ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken about-- about a year ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1994. This is my youngest son, Rabbi Allen Schwartz's family [NON-ENGLISH]. This is four children only-- now he has another child, Esther, the youngest, and here is Chana, their mother, Elissa, a little Elly, and Moishe, and Shonnie. This was taken about, I would say, five years ago-- five years ago. This is my latest and youngest granddaughter. Her name is Esther. This is my youngest son, Rabbi Allen, and Elissa Schwartz's youngest daughter. She is now two and a half years old. She talks and is a very happy child, the youngest of our grandchildren. This is the house where I was born. I went to visit the house to look how it looks when I was in Lutsk in 1989. And I would not dare to go to see the house myself. But I went to some friends. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it broke my heart to see the apartment and the house where my parents lived, and I grew up. But there's nobody left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how do I look? I don't know-- I should have looked in the mirror.

speaker: interviewer
text: No more. No more. That's my wife, Sarah. My oldest daughter, Marlene. And my oldest granddaughter, Laurie. Now, what do you want to say, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry all our other grandchildren aren't here with us. Unfortunately, we're very busy today. But thank God they're fine. And my grand-- my daughter-in-law is feeling much better. So this is just part of the family. But we're very proud of all of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'll say that Dad, I know this must have been very hard for you to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was emotional a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad that it was done and the story was told. I always felt that it should be told. And I feel that, truly, you have done your part in helping for peace, and to see that something like this never happens again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would just like to say that I know I speak for all the other grandchildren that we have all learned a lot from all of your stories. And we've heard them all. I'm sure there are lots of things that you've left out, that you've forgotten. And we've all just learned so much from it. And we will never forget anything that he's ever told us about. We feel like we've known everyone that he's spoken about, his parents, his brothers and sisters. And we feel like they've always been with us through all of the years. And we-- we truly have-- just everything he's ever told us we value so much. And we love you very much, Zayde.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you. I love you.
